title-group.article-title: Circadian synchronization between diet and gut microbiota in modulating host's health

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: Extensive research has established a complex relationship between the host, diet, and gut microbiota. Recent studies highlight the critical role of the host circadian clock in maintaining health. Notably, diet and gut microbiota composition have been shown to modulate circadian clock gene expression. Here, we summarize the influences of diet, gut microbiota, and the circadian clock on host health, as well as how diet and gut microbiota regulate the circadian clock. In particular, we focus on the mechanisms by which diet and gut microbiota regulate the circadian clock. Given the widespread impact of diet, gut microbiota, and the circadian clock on host health, understanding their complex relationship is a critical aspect of maintaining host homeostasis and health.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: To anticipate daily environmental changes and the light-dark cycle, nearly all organisms have evolved corresponding rhythms, known as Circadian Clocks (CCs).1,2 At the heart of the molecular circadian clock in animals is a transcription–translation feedback loop (TTFL), which takes approximately 24 hours to complete.3 This core oscillator comprises positive regulatory elements, negative regulatory elements, and auxiliary loops that confer stability and fine-tuning.

The positive regulatory elements consist of the Circadian Locomotor Output Cycles Kaput (CLOCK) and Brain and Muscle ARNT-Like 1 (BMAL1) proteins. They form a heterodimer that binds to E-box cis-regulatory elements in target gene promoters, driving the transcription of a suite of clock-controlled genes, including the negative regulators.3 The negative regulatory elements include the PERIOD (PER1, PER2, PER3) and CRYPTOCHROME (CRY1, CRY2) proteins. Following their CLOCK-BMAL1-mediated transcription, these proteins accumulate in the cytoplasm, undergo post-translational modifications, and assemble into multimeric complexes. Upon nuclear translocation, the PER-CRY complexes directly interact with the CLOCK-BMAL1 heterodimer, inhibiting its transcriptional activity and thereby repressing their own expression and that of other target genes.3 Auxiliary regulators provide an additional layer of rhythmic control. For instance, the expression of Rev-erbα/β (Nr1d1/2) and Rorα/β/γ is activated by the core loop. Nuclear Receptor Subfamily 1, Group D, Member 1/2 (REV-ERBα/β) proteins function as transcriptional repressors by binding to ROR response elements (ROREs) in the Bmal1 promoter, while RAR-related orphan receptor (ROR) proteins act as activators by competing for the same sites. This antagonistic interplay between REV-ERBs and RORs generates secondary oscillations in Bmal1 transcription.3

Beyond transcriptional feedback, post-translational modifications (PTMs), particularly phosphorylation, are critical for timing and precision. For example, BMAL1 is phosphorylated by casein kinase Iδ/ε (CKIδ/ε) and mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK). MAPK-mediated phosphorylation at multiple sites attenuates the transcriptional activity of the CLOCK-BMAL1 heterodimer.4 Epigenetic mechanisms are integral to circadian transcriptional output. rhythmic chromatin accessibility, dynamically regulated by core clock proteins and coordinated with histone modifications and 3D chromatin architecture, ensures temporally precise gene expression.5 Furthermore, catalytic inactivation of the histone methyltransferase mixed lineage leukemia 3 (MLL3) dampens oscillations in the promoters of core clock genes (Bmal1, mCry1, mPer2, Rev-erbα [Nr1d1]), highlighting the importance of rhythmic histone methylation for robust transcriptional cycling.6 Translational regulation is another key node. Upstream open reading frames (uORFs) can rhythmically repress Clock mRNA translation, influencing circadian period, photic entrainment, and physiological adaptation.7 The formation of dynamic biomolecular condensates via liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS) plays a crucial role in maintaining circadian precision and amplitude. The proteins Ataxin 2(ATXN2) and Ataxin 2 Like (ATXN2L) form coordinated, rhythmic biomolecular condensates that selectively recruit translational machinery and specific mRNA transcripts, enabling timed translation of core clock genes.8

Systemic signals, notably diet and metabolites, entrain peripheral clocks. Dietary/metabolic signals shape the rhythmicity of the gut microbiota, which in turn modulates oscillations in host metabolism via microbially derived metabolites and components.9 The host circadian system and the gut microbiota collaboratively integrate environmental cues, particularly diet, to synchronize coordinated metabolic responses.10 Thus, the diet-microbiota-host circadian axis is a critical determinant of host health. Diet and the microbiota exert profound effects on organismal physiology largely through the modulation of host clock gene expression, underscoring that the rhythmic expression of clock genes is fundamental to maintaining health and normal physiological function (Figure 1).

fig.label: Figure 1.

caption.p: Crosstalk among diet, gut microbiota, and the host circadian clock: implications for organismal health. (Arg, arginine; CBAs, Conjugated Bile Acids; Fa, Fatty Acid; G, Glucose; SBAs, secondary bile acids; SCFAs, Short-Chain Fatty Acids; Spd, Spermidine; Trp, tryptophan; TTFL, transcription–translation feedback loop; UBAs, Unconjugated Bile Acids) The gut microbial metabolites act as central mediators in the diet–host–microbiota dialog, linking nutritional inputs to organismal circadian physiology. Dietary components are processed by host metabolism to modulate microbial communities, which in turn produce bioactive metabolites that regulate molecular clock function. Conversely, the production and turnover of these metabolites exhibit circadian oscillations, revealing a bidirectional regulatory circuit between microbial metabolism and host circadian timing.

fig.alt-text: A diagram shows diet, gut microbiota, and host circadian clock interactions affecting organismal health.
fig.long-desc: The diagram shows diet, gut microbiota, and host circadian clock interactions affecting organismal health. The left side is labeled Diet, showing polysaccharide, protein, and triglycerides. A large pink arrow labeled Host metabolic processes affect the metabolism of gut microbiota points from Host Metabolism to Microbiota Metabolism. A large blue arrow labeled Gut microbiota derived metabolites influence host metabolism points from Microbiota Metabolism back to Host Metabolism, forming a central loop. Host Metabolism is at the top center, showing chemical structures for Arg, Trp, Fa, and G. Microbiota Metabolism is at the bottom center, showing SBAs, SCFAs, UBAs, and Spd.

sec.p: In this review, we focus on the impact of diet and gut microbiota on host health, specifically how they regulate host clock genes to influence physiological function. Understanding the intricate connections between diet, gut microbiota, host’s circadian clocks, and health offers insights for developing nutritional and microbial therapies targeting diseases caused by circadian disruption.

sec.title: Circadian regulation of host homeostasis and health
sec.p: CCs represent a fundamental life trait, regulating essential physiological processes including immune system function, thermoregulation, hormone secretion, and metabolic activity.11,12 Disruption of these rhythms contributes to the pathogenesis and exacerbation of various diseases, such as obesity, metabolic syndrome, cancer, type 2 diabetes (T2D), and gastrointestinal disorders.13-15 In animals, the molecular circadian clock governs the expression of output genes throughout the body, thereby temporally regulating the activity and function of diverse cells and organs.16

sec.title: Circadian orchestration of intestinal homeostasis
sec.p: Intestinal epithelial cells (IECs), positioned at the interface of luminal nutrients and microbiota, execute critical barrier, immune, and absorptive/secretory functions,17-19 nearly all of which demonstrate circadian oscillations.20-24 The IEC-intrinsic circadian clock is robust, driving rhythmic expression of 3–10% of detectable transcripts in murine and human gut.25 Consequently, maintaining IEC circadian homeostasis is essential for intestinal integrity.

Group 3 innate lymphoid cells (ILC3s), key mediators of intestinal immunity,26 promote epithelial antimicrobial peptide expression via Interleukin-22 (IL-22) secretion,27,28 thereby reinforcing barrier function. ILC3s require cell-autonomous clock signaling to sustain their circadian rhythmicity.29,30 Disruption of core clock genes impairs ILC3 chromatin accessibility; REV-ERBα deficiency specifically compromises ILC3 development, reducing Natural Killer Cell P46 Receptor (NKp46) ILC3 abundance and RORγt expression, ultimately impairing ILC3 maturation and function.31 This cascade undermines intestinal immune-barrier integrity.

A critical link exists between inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) and circadian disruption.32 IBD—a chronic inflammatory disorder of the gastrointestinal tract33 —exhibits profound clock gene dysregulation, with 3-fold reduced expression of BMAL1, CLOCK, PER1/2 and CRY1/2 in patient mucosal tissues and peripheral blood.34 Murine studies confirm enhanced colitis susceptibility in Rorα- or Bmal1-deficient models,35,36 mechanistically attributed to RORα loss driving hyperactive NF-κB signaling and suppressed IEC regenerative capacity.35

The molecular clock critically governs intestinal tissue renewal. While initial studies suggested non-rhythmic proliferation,37 subsequent work revealed circadian oscillations in IEC and mucosal cell proliferation.38,39 BMAL1 regulates this process by controlling circadian Tumor Necrosis Factor (TNF) and C-C Motif Chemokine Ligand 2 (CCL2) expression.40 The BMAL1:CLOCK heterodimer binds E-box motifs in the CCL2 promoter41; CCL2- C-C Chemokine Receptor 2 (CCR2) signaling promotes proliferation.42 TNF exhibits circadian rhythmicity under clock control43 and activates pro-proliferative NF-κB and c-Jun N-terminal Kinase (JNK) pathways.44 BMAL1 orchestrates IEC-intrinsic TNF oscillations; TNF drives proliferation via JNK-c-JUN activation. BMAL1 ablation disrupts TNF-induced P21 expression,40,44 impairing G1/S transition and proliferative rhythmicity. Further studies reveal that diminished PER2 expression reduces phosphorylation of Drp1 at Ser637 (p-Drp1S637). As p-Drp1S637 serves as a critical regulator of mitochondrial fusion, its attenuation triggers mitochondrial fragmentation, compromising mitochondrial energy metabolism and consequently ATP production. This cascade ultimately suppresses colonic epithelial cell proliferation.45 Mechanistically, the core clock gene PER2 sustains mitochondrial energy metabolism and ATP generation by governing p-Drp1S637-dependent mitochondrial fusion. Through this pathway, PER2 drives colonic epithelial proliferation, facilitates tissue repair, and ameliorates experimental colitis. Collectively, these findings establish that core clock genes govern intestinal immunity, injury progression, and repair mechanisms—reinforcing the indispensable role of IEC circadian rhythms in gut health.

sec.title: Systemic physiological regulation by the circadian clock
sec.p: Beyond its critical functions in the gut, the biological clock exerts profound systemic influences on diverse physiological processes. Endocrine signals exhibit robust 24-hour oscillations.46,47 These diurnal rhythms are not merely responsive to external cues but are intrinsically governed by the core molecular circadian clock.48 Disruption of clock genes impairs rhythmic hormone secretion, compromising systemic homeostasis.49-51 Clock-mutant mice display attenuated diurnal feeding rhythms, hyperphagia, obesity, and metabolic syndrome characterized by hyperleptinemia, hyperlipidemia, hepatic steatosis, and hyperglycemia, with insufficient compensatory insulin production—hallmarks of T2D.52 Per1/2 knockout abolishes circadian corticosterone fluctuations, maintaining constitutively low levels.53 Bmal1−/− mice exhibit downregulated Growth Hormone (GH)-mediated Janus Kinase-Signal Transducer and Activator of Transcription (JAK-STAT) signaling, evidenced by reduced tyrosine phosphorylation of STAT5 and impaired GH responsiveness in vivo (liver) and in vitro (hepatocytes).54 Pancreatic islets from Clock or Bmal1 knockout mice show reduced insulin secretion during feeding, blunted glucose-stimulated insulin release, decreased fasting insulin levels, and islet atrophy.55 Collectively, these findings establish that core clock genes govern essential endocrine functions.

Genome-wide combinatorial analyzes reveal significant enrichment of clock components on promoters of metabolic genes, particularly those regulating carbohydrate and lipid metabolism.56,57 Bmal1 deficiency abolishes rhythms in blood glucose and triglycerides, dampens gluconeogenesis, and disrupts hepatic/aortic Phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase (PEPCK) (a rate-limiting gluconeogenic enzyme) oscillations.58 Bmal1−/− embryonic fibroblasts fail to differentiate into adipocytes; adenoviral Bmal1 rescue restores adipogenesis, while its overexpression induces lipogenic genes (e.g., Fasn, Aacs, Hmgcr).59 Cry1/2 deletion causes glucose intolerance and constitutively elevated corticosterone, with enhanced hepatic glucocorticoid transactivation. Cry1/2 double-knockout (DKO) mice fail to suppress glucocorticoids during daytime and exhibit impaired glucose clearance,60 indicating CRY-mediated restraint of GR activity prevents gluconeogenic excess. Cry1/2 DKO mice further develop hyperinsulinemia and increased adiposity.61 Rev-erbα/β DKO mice exhibit hypertriglyceridemia, hyperglycemia, decreased free fatty acids, elevated plasma Low-Density Lipoprotein (LDL)/High-Density Lipoprotein (HDL), and dysregulated rhythms of LXR/FXR/SREBP targets.62,63 Hepatic Rev-erbα knockout reduces Histone Deacetylase 3 (HDAC3) recruitment, permitting constitutive lipid gene expression and steatosis; Rev-erbα abundance recruits HDAC3 to suppress lipogenesis.64 The mechanism explains how the absence of HDAC3 or Rev-erbα triggers persistent expression of lipogenic genes, leading to excessive lipid synthesis and consequent hepatic steatosis. Rev-erbα−/− skeletal muscle shows reduced mitochondrial DNA content, increased autophagic flux, and diminished exercise capacity.65 Hepatic Rev-erbα knockout reduces HDAC3 recruitment, permitting constitutive lipid gene expression and steatosis; Rev-erbα abundance recruits HDAC3 to suppress lipogenesis. Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide (NAD⁺)—a metabolic coenzyme synthesized from tryptophan (Trp), niacin, and nicotinamide—oscillates in a clock-dependent manner. The level of NAD⁺ reflects the cellular energy status (as indicated by the NAD⁺/NADH ratio), serving as a critical sensor of metabolic state. Clock mutant and arrhythmic Cry1/2 DKO mice exhibit dampened NAD⁺ rhythms, while Clock:Bmal1-deficient mice show reduced hepatic/plasma Nicotinamide Phosphoribosyltransferase (Nampt) and NAD⁺ levels,66,67 establishing CLOCK:BMAL1 regulation of NAD⁺ synthesis via NAMPT. Elevated NAD⁺ enhances Silent Information Regulator 1 (SIRT1) deacetylase activity, modulating glucose/lipid metabolism and insulin secretion.68 Overexpression of Bmal1/Clock in hepatocytes promotes SIRT1 accumulation, whereas their disruption reduces SIRT1 expression.69

The molecular clock additionally orchestrates immune regulation. Inflammatory cytokines exhibit circadian oscillations in both transcriptional dynamics and secretion patterns.70 Most immune cells autonomously express core clock-regulatory genes,12,71 which critically govern functional outputs including: Cellular migration and phagocytic activity, Immunometabolic programming, Signal transduction cascades, Inflammatory responses, Innate immune sensing and adaptive immunity.41,72-74 Endogenous circadian timing coordinates multilayered rhythms within the immune system, essential for maintaining immunological homeostasis and host defense.71,75,76 Genetic disruption of clock components compromises immune competence.75,77 Cry-deficient mice demonstrate constitutive Protein Kinase A (PKA) activation that enhances p65 phosphorylation at Ser276, driving NF-κB hyperactivation and elevated proinflammatory cytokine production.78 Cry DKO mice exhibit 5-6-fold increased serum Immunoglobulin G (IgG) titers and robust anti-nuclear antibodies, indicating predisposition to autoimmune pathogenesis.79 These findings establish CRY as a pivotal regulator of immune homeostasis. Rev-erbα−/− mice show elevated Nuclear Factor, Interleukin-3 Regulated (Nfil3) expression and impaired T helper 17 (Th17) differentiation, phenocopied by Clock19/19 -mediated REV-ERBα suppression.80 The circadian-immune axis gates Th17 commitment through the REV-ERBα-NFIL3-RORγt transcriptional cascade. RORα deficiency reduces Interleukin-17 (IL-17)/Interleukin-17F (IL-17F) production, while its overexpression enhances their expression.81 This further demonstrates that the circadian clock modulates immune competence by regulating the differentiation of immune cells.

Collectively, circadian machinery modulates organismal physiology through integrated control of endocrine signaling, metabolic flux, and immune function. Given this foundational role in health maintenance, we propose to investigate: How dietary nutrients and gut microbiota impact the transcriptional regulation and oscillatory dynamics of core clock genes, and through what mechanistic pathways.

sec.title: Nutrient-sensing pathways converge on the circadian clock
sec.p: A hierarchical network of central and peripheral clocks maintains circadian physiology: the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) in the hypothalamus acts as the master pacemaker, synchronizing internal time with environmental light cues.2 while peripheral oscillators reside in nearly all tissues and are primarily entrained by feeding.82-84 Beyond its role as a zeitgeber, feeding influences immunity and acts as a key driver of gut microbiota composition and function.85 Diet is crucial for health both nutritionally and functionally, with the latter encompassing nutrient-mediated regulation of clock gene expression.86

Substantial evidence indicates that dietary nutrients modulate the expression of core clock genes.86-88 Dietary carbohydrates are hydrolyzed into absorbable mono- and oligosaccharides. Intriguingly, the phase and period of circadian clock gene expression in cultured cells depend directly on glucose concentration in the medium,89 establishing glucose as a peripheral signal regulating cellular circadian rhythms. In vivo, postprandial hyperglycemia elevates insulin levels.90 Insulin then signals through the phosphoinositide 3-kinase (PI3K) pathway to modulate Forkhead Box O3 (FOXO3) activity. FOXO3 directly binds two Daf binding elements (DBEs) within the Clock gene promoter, thereby regulating its transcription.91 Furthermore, glucose levels influence the activity of energy sensors like SIRT1 and Adenosine Monophosphate-Activated Protein Kinase (AMPK) via metabolites, subsequently regulating the expression of clock genes such as Bmal1.92

Oscillations in NAD⁺ levels constitute a key regulatory step governing rhythmicity in metabolic tissues and the peripheral clock.66 Aging coincides with circadian disruption and declining NAD⁺.93 Dietary L-tryptophan is catabolized via indoleamine-2, 3-dioxygenase (IDO)/tryptophan-2, 3-dioxygenase (TDO) and downstream enzymes to quinolinic acid (QA), which is converted to nicotinic acid mononucleotide (NAMN) by Quinolinate Phosphoribosyltransferase (QPRT) and ultimately to NAD⁺. The salvage pathway converts nicotinamide (NAM) to NMN via NAMPT, followed by conversion to NAD⁺ by NMNAT isoforms.94,95 As noted earlier, NAMPT expression is regulated by the CLOCK: BMAL1 heterodimer. This regulation is bidirectional: NAD⁺-dependent deacetylase SIRT1 reciprocally modulates clock gene activity.96 NAD⁺ depletion reduces SIRT1 transcription and protein synthesis; SIRT1 deficiency attenuates or phase-shifts clock gene expression, significantly increases PER2 nuclear accumulation, decreases PER1, CRY1, and CRY2 levels,93 and causes persistently elevated acetylation of histone H3 (Lys9/Lys14) and BMAL1 (Lys537), abolishing circadian oscillations.97 Thus, NAD⁺ levels regulate CLOCK: BMAL1 acetylation and target gene transcription via SIRT1.66,67

Fluctuations in the intracellular NAD⁺/NADH redox state directly govern clock transcriptional activity. Elevated NADH potently enhances the DNA-binding capacity of CLOCK:BMAL1 and NPAS2:BMAL1 heterodimers (EC₅₀ for NADH ≈ 6.3 mM).98 NADH levels rise postprandially due to nutrient catabolism outside mitochondria, indicating that nutrient intake influences NADH to modulate clock gene transcription via this mechanism.

Proteins in the diet can be broken down into various amino acids, such as Trp and arginine (Arg). Trp metabolism integrates environmental cues to regulate circadian homeostasis.99 Dietary Trp catabolism yields metabolites (e.g., 6-Formylindolo[3, 2-b]carbazole [FICZ], Inositol 1, 4, 5-trisphosphate [I3P]) that activate the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AHR).100,101 AHR activation alters clock gene transcripts and represses molecular rhythms.102-105 AHR forms complexes with BMAL1, disrupting CLOCK-BMAL1 binding to E-boxes and suppressing Per1 expression.106 Trp also serves as a precursor for melatonin, a pineal hormone synchronizing peripheral clocks. Melatonin binds membrane receptors Melatonin Receptor 1 (MT1)/Melatonin Receptor 2 (MT2),107 activating downstream pathways (e.g., Protein Kinase C [PKC]/MAPK via MT2) that directly regulate core clock gene expression, enhance BMAL1/CLOCK-E-box binding, and promote Per and Cry transcription.108 Dietary melatonin or melatonin-promoting compounds can thus influence clock gene expression.109

Dietary Arg and its metabolites significantly impact circadian rhythms. Arg binds specific sensors (e.g., Transmembrane 4 L six family member 5 [TM4SF5]) and signals through the GAP Activity TOward Rags 2 (GATOR2)- GTPase-Activating Protein (GAP) for Rag family 1 (GATOR1)- KPTN-ITFG2-C12orf66-SZT2-TELO2 complex (KICSTOR)- Ras-related GTP-binding proteins (Rag GTPases) axis to mechanistic target of rapamycin complex (mTORC1).110 Arg sufficiency recruits mTORC1 to Rheb-containing lysosomal surfaces via Rag GTPases,111,112 where TM4SF5 translocates from the plasma membrane to sense Arg, ultimately activating mTORC1/S6 Kinase 1 (S6K1).113 Arg can regulate mTORC1 signaling,114 Rapamycin (an mTOR inhibitor) reduces circadian amplitude and phase-advances Clock, Cry1, and Per1 mRNA rhythms.115 Conversely, mTORC1 hyperactivation (e.g., via Tuberous Sclerosis Complex 1/2 [TSC1/2] deletion) elevates BMAL1 protein synthesis, leading to aberrant BMAL1-CLOCK transcriptional activity and disrupted rhythm amplitude/phase.116 Furthermore, mTORC1 effector Ribosomal Protein S6K1 directly phosphorylates BMAL1 at serine 42 (S42), a critical modification regulating BMAL1's translational function.117 Thus, mTORC1 acts as a nutrient/energy sensor, transducing metabolic signals into translational control of circadian protein synthesis via BMAL1 phosphorylation. mTORC1 also influences Signal Transducer and Activator of Transcription 3 (STAT3) transcriptional function (directly or via transcription factors).118-120 STAT3 and mTORC1 jointly regulate RORα/γt expression. RORα, a core clock component,121 directly regulates Bmal1. Embryonic fibroblasts reveal Arg-dependent RORα regulation.122 Polyamines, downstream of Arg metabolism, also exert circadian effects.

Spermidine (Spd), a major arginine-derived polyamine, critically influences cellular function and metabolism.123,124 While widely studied for its geroprotective properties,125-127 Spd also interfaces with the molecular clock. Spd promotes PER2-CRY1 heterodimer formation, modulating cellular rhythms.128 It regulates clock gene acetylation by inhibiting acetyltransferase E1A Binding Protein p300 (EP300) activity, thereby influencing CLOCK and BMAL1 function.129-131 Spd also activates SIRT1 to promote histone deacetylation and circadian regulation.132 Spd levels bidirectionally modulate circadian period: low levels lengthen, while high levels shorten rhythms.128 Reciprocally, the clock governs Spd biosynthesis; genes encoding rate-limiting enzymes (e.g., Ornithine Decarboxylase [Odc], Adenosylmethionine Decarboxylase [Amd], Spermidine Synthase [Srm]) are clock-controlled, with CLOCK-BMAL1 driving their transcription via E-box elements.133 Notably, Spd levels and synthetic capacity decline with age.134-136 Both Spd and NAD⁺ synthesis are clock-regulated, and both feedback to modulate clock function, suggesting a reciprocal relationship that fine-tunes and reinforces circadian robustness.

Consequently, current understanding links mechanisms counteracting aging to the circadian clock. Studies indicate that cellular senescence disrupts the homeostatic molecular circadian system.137-141 Spd can attenuate cellular senescence through the induction of autophagy,142 a process itself exhibiting robust circadian rhythmicity.143,144 Spd activates AMPK, which in turn promotes autophagy by inhibiting the mechanistic target of rapamycin (mTOR) signaling pathway.145 mTOR, a key regulator of autophagy,146 inhibits autophagosome formation when active by directly phosphorylating core autophagy-initiating components, including the UNC-51-like Kinase 1/2 (ULK1/2) complex, transcription factor EB (TFEB), and death-associated protein 1 (DAP1).147-150 Furthermore, phosphorylated TFEB promotes the expression of Rev-erbα.151 Notably, TFEB regulates Rev-erbα expression independently of and upstream of the core BMAL1/CLOCK oscillator.151 The Rev-erbα protein binds to regulatory regions of several autophagy-related genes and represses their transcription.152 Spd counteracts this by enhancing the formation of the PER2-CRY1 heterodimer, thereby suppressing Rev-erbα expression– thus representing a potential pathway for modulating circadian autophagy. Rev-erbα functions dually as a core circadian repressor and an autophagy inhibitor.115,153-155 These concepts are illustrated in Figure 2.

fig.label: Figure 2.

caption.p: Spd’s Involvement in Modulating Cellular Autophagy via the Circadian Clock. (Ac, Acetylated proteins; Atg 14, Autophagy-related 14; PGC-1α, Peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma coactivator 1-alpha; Rev-erbα/β, Nr1d1/2; Nuclear Receptor Subfamily 1, Group D, Member 1/2) Spd modulates cellular autophagy by regulating energy pathways and the molecular circadian clock, while also being subject to regulation of its endogenous biosynthesis. Spd activates the AMPK signaling pathway and inhibits the mTOR signaling pathway, thereby relieving the inhibition of TFEB, a key transcription factor in autophagy, and promoting its nuclear translocation. After translocating into the nucleus, TFEB drives the expression of autophagy-related genes, which in turn promotes autophagosome formation and enhances the cellular capacity for self-cleaning and renewal. Spd can affect the core oscillator of the circadian clock. It acts on the ROREs in the promoter regions of target genes by regulating the balance between RORα and REV-ERBα, a pair of competitive nuclear receptors. Meanwhile, by influencing the binding of the CLOCK/BMAL1 heterodimer to E-box elements, it bidirectionally regulates the transcription of downstream clock genes (e.g., PER1/2/3, CRY1/2, Nr1d1/2), which may stabilize or strengthen cellular circadian rhythms. As a core component of polyamine metabolism, Spd involves key enzymes such as Odc, Amd1, and Srm in its synthesis and catabolism. Additionally, it enhances the deacetylase activity of SIRT1 by increasing intracellular NAD⁺ levels, potentially through the activation of NAMPT. Activated SIRT1 can further deacetylate and activate metabolic regulators such as PGC-1α, and may affect gene expression via epigenetic modifications, thereby linking cellular metabolic status to physiological processes including anti-aging and stress resistance.

fig.alt-text: A diagram shows spermidine modulating cellular autophagy and circadian clock via multiple pathways.
fig.long-desc: The diagram shows spermidine modulating cellular autophagy and the circadian clock via multiple pathways. Spermidine is at the upper left. Arrows from spermidine point to AMPK, P300, and ROR alpha. AMPK leads to mTOR, which leads to DAP1, UK1/2, and TFEB. TFEB phosphorylation leads to REV-ERB alpha, which leads to autophagosome genes. Autophagosome genes lead to mRNA. Autophagosome is also shown. P300 leads to CRY PER, which leads to BMAL1 CLOCK. BMAL1 CLOCK interacts with E box and RORE. RORE interacts with ROR alpha and REVERBs. ROR alpha leads to PGC1 alpha, which leads to SIRT1. SIRT1 leads to NAD plus, which leads to NAMPT. NAMPT leads to SIRT1. Spermidine also leads to ODC, AMD, SRM, which leads to spermidine. Text labels include ATG 14, ODC, AMD, SRM, PER1/2/3, CRY1 slash 2, R E V E R B alpha slash beta, and N A M P T. The diagram illustrates the complex interplay of spermidine in regulating cellular processes.

sec.p: Impairment of its rhythmic expression exacerbates autophagic dysfunction, compromising the clearance of senescent cells. Autophagy activation facilitates the removal of damaged proteins and organelles, thereby ensuring the proper circadian expression and oscillation of core clock genes.

Furthermore, the breakdown of dietary nutrients is modulated by the circadian oscillations of the gut microbiota and the host circadian clock network (governing absorption, transport, storage, and utilization), as well as rhythmic host metabolic processes (e.g., protein turnover).156-158 Table 1 reveals robust circadian oscillations of nutrient metabolites—including Spd, Trp, and short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs)—across multiple host compartments: blood, gut lumen, saliva, and liver. Supporting this, 60 metabolites exhibit significant circadian oscillations in wild-type mice, whereas only 7 do so in Clock-deficient mice,159 underscoring the dominant role of the host clock in governing metabolic rhythms.

sec.title: Gut microbiota critically regulates host circadian rhythms through multimodal signaling pathways
sec.p: The gut microbiota and their metabolites exert a significant impact on the diurnal epigenetics and transcriptome of host tissues.163 In studies involving conventionally raised (CV) mice, germ-free (GF) mice, and antibiotic-treated (ABX) mice, it was found that both ABX and GF mice exhibit disrupted rhythms of clock gene expression,170 indicating that the gut microbiota exerts a significant influence on the host’s molecular clock. Gut microbes can affect host gene expression through their metabolites and breakdown products.171

When gut microbes are degraded, they release components (such as lipopolysaccharide [LPS], flagellin, and peptidoglycan) that can activate Toll-like receptors (TLRs) 2, 4, and 5.172-174 Activation of TLRs triggers Myeloid differentiation primary response 88 (MyD88)-dependent or MyD88-independent pathways, thereby activating inflammatory signaling pathways such as NF-κB and MAPK.175,176 NF-κB directly binds to the transactivation domain (TAD) of BMAL1, competitively displacing CRY1 and cAMP Response Element-Binding Protein Binding Protein (CBP)/EP300 and inhibiting the transcriptional activation function of the BMAL1/CLOCK complex. This reprograms the E-box-driven clock gene expression network and ultimately affects cellular circadian rhythms.177-180 Under LPS stimulation, TLR2 and Nucleotide-Binding Oligomerization Domain-Containing Protein 2 (NOD2) are activated, influencing the activation of downstream NF-κB signaling. Subsequently, dendritic cells secrete the cytokine IL-23, which acts on the IL-23 receptor of Th17 cells to activate the JAK-STAT pathway and STAT3, affecting the expression of RORα.121 Gut microbe-derived LPS can also induce activation of REV-ERBα, enhancing repression of BMAL1 and exacerbating circadian disruption.181 At the same time, LPS strongly induces the expression of the pro-inflammatory microRNA miR-155.182 miR-155 directly suppresses BMAL1 mRNA and protein expression by targeting the 3′ untranslated region (UTR) of BMAL1.183 Intriguingly, an experiment showed that treating GF mice with flagellin and LPS leads to a decrease in Rev-erbα expression.184 The transcription factor STAT3 is a key downstream effector of the IL-22 receptor.185 Activation of the IL-22R leads to phosphorylation of STAT3, after which STAT3 binds to the Rev-erbα promoter and represses Rev-erbα expression184 (Figure 3). Under LPS stimulation, TLR activation causes a shift in cellular energy metabolism toward glycolysis,186 thereby altering the NAD+/NADH ratio. NAD+ is a coenzyme for sirtuins, and changes in NAD+ levels can affect the activity of SIRT1 (an NAD+-dependent deacetylase).187 SIRT1 can deacetylate the PER and BMAL1 proteins, thereby influencing clock gene expression96,97 (Figure 3). TLR signaling also induces the generation of reactive oxygen species and activates oxidative stress pathways such as Nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (Nrf2), indirectly affecting clock gene expression.188 Another study found that peripheral injection of LPS into the peritoneal cavity can transiently suppress the mRNA expression of Per2 and D-box binding protein (Dbp), and reduce the amplitude of clock gene expression in the rat SCN.189

fig.label: Figure 3.

caption.p: Mechanisms of circadian rhythm regulation by gut microbiota and metabolites. (DC, Dendritic cells; Dbp, D-box binding protein; G⁺ bacteria, Gram-positive bacteria; G⁻ bacteria, Gram-positive bacteria; H3K27ac, Histone H3 Lysine 27 Acetylation; H3K9ac, Histone H3 Lysine 9 Acetylation; IL-22R, Interleukin-22 receptor; Myd88, Myeloid differentiation primary response 88; Nfil3, Nuclear Factor, Interleukin 3 Regulated; Rev-erbα, Nr1d1; SBAs, Secondary bile acids) Gut microbiota-derived metabolites, including SCFAs and bile acids (BAs), regulate host circadian rhythms through multiple pathways. BAs directly influence the rhythmicity of IECs, while these and other metabolites enter systemic circulation to affect distal clocks, such as in the liver. Furthermore, SCFAs modulate the molecular clock by elevating NAD+ levels and inhibiting histone deacetylases (HDAC). An additional regulatory mechanism involves IL-23 pathway-mediated modulation of the molecular clock by microbial metabolites.

fig.alt-text: A multi-panel diagram shows gut microbiota and metabolites regulating circadian rhythms through multiple pathways.
fig.long-desc: The multi-panel diagram shows gut microbiota and metabolites regulating circadian rhythms through multiple pathways. The diagram is arranged with a liver and other organs at the top left, a blood vessel running horizontally across the middle, and intestinal epithelial cells with a lumen at the bottom right. At the top left, a liver contains Nfil3 and Bmall, and Cry 1 slash 2 and Dbp. These connect to clock genes and other organs. Clock genes influence B M A L 1, C R Y 1, and P E R 1 transcription, leading to circadian rhythms alignment. P E R 2, B M A L 1, P E R 1, C R Y 1, and B M A L 1 promoter regions are shown. S I R T 1 and N A D plus are also depicted. Butyrate inhibits H D A C, which in turn affects histone H 3 K 27 a c and H 3 K 9 a c. Below the blood vessel, S B A, U B A, and bile S C F A s are shown in the lumen. Gram negative flagellated bacteria release L P S, which activates T L R. G plus bacteria are also present in the lumen. D C dendritic cells are activated by T L R and Myd88, leading to I L 23 production. I L C 3 cells are also shown, producing I L 22, which binds to I L 22 R. I L 22 R activates S T A T 3, which influences R e v e r b alpha and Nfil3. R e v e r b alpha and Nfil3 are linked to lipid homeostasis and I L C 3 slash T h 17 development.

sec.p: The gut microbiota can convert dietary proteins into polyamines such as Spd,190 which can influence clock gene expression. Additionally, it has been found that gut microbes can synthesize melatonin from tryptophan.191 This suggests that gut microbes might influence the host’s circadian rhythms by producing melatonin from dietary tryptophan. Gut microbes can also convert dietary polysaccharides into SCFAs, and microbiota-derived SCFAs or lactate can regulate the phase of the host peripheral clock in vivo.192

Butyrate secreted by gut microbes is known for its ability to inhibit HDACs.193 Studies have shown that HDAC inhibition by butyrate increases histone acetylation, leading to a more open chromatin configuration that promotes clock gene transcription. This epigenetic regulation can enhance clock gene transcription in peripheral tissues.5,160,194-198 It has also been reported that the expression of HDAC3 is significantly reduced in GF mice, but colonization with commensal bacteria can restore HDAC3 expression.199 Deletion of HDAC3 disrupts the expression of circadian genes and results in low levels of BMAL1 and CRY proteins, whereas overexpression of HDAC3 enhances the transcriptional amplitude of Per2 and Dbp.200

In addition, SCFAs can activate specific G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) that are expressed on various cell types (including immune cells and IECs).201 Microbial metabolites such as SBAs and SCFAs also impact the expression of host clock genes. For example, deoxycholic acid (DCA) and chenodeoxycholic acid (CDCA) are UBAs derived from the gut microbiota, and their oral administration alters the expression of clock and clock-regulating genes in the ileum, colon, and liver of mice,202 suggesting that SBAs can influence host clock gene expression. SBAs are natural ligands of the nuclear receptor Farnesoid X Receptor (FXR).203-205 Upon activation, FXR forms a heterodimer with the retinoid X receptor (RXR) and binds to FXR response elements (FXRE) in the promoters of target genes, directly regulating the transcription of clock genes such as BMAL1 and CLOCK.204,206 RXRα, in a ligand-dependent manner, can bind to the basic Helix-Loop-Helix protein (bHLH) proteins Neuronal PAS Domain Protein 2 (NPAS2) and CLOCK (but not BMAL1) and inhibit the transcriptional activation of clock gene promoters by the CLOCK/NPAS2-BMAL1 heterodimer in vitro.205,207

In addition to regulating the molecular circadian clock of the host organism, the composition and function of the gut microbiota are not static but instead exhibit circadian rhythmic oscillations.208,209 These oscillations are likely linked to host physiological processes—including metabolism, immune responses, and gut barrier function—primarily because the gut microbiota critically influences these processes, and its rhythmicity impacts specific host functions. Table 2 further reveals gut microbiota exhibiting circadian rhythms within the host organism, such as Firmicutes and Muribaculaceae. Rather than maintaining stable compositions, these taxa display diurnal oscillations.

tr.td: Indole, butyric acid, secondary bile acids (DCA, LCA)
Propionic acid, Acetate lactate, succinic acid
Elaidate, trans-C18:1n9, Palmitate, C16:0, Myristate, C14:0, Stearate, C18:0, Margarate, C17:0
Acetate lactate, propionic acid, succinic acid

sec.p: The effect of the gut microbiota on the host's molecular circadian clock is not unidirectional; circadian rhythms also profoundly shape the composition of the gut microbiota. Disruption of the host clock perturbs microbial community structure.210 For instance, Per1/2 knockout mice lose feeding rhythmicity, exhibit disrupted microbial oscillations, and develop metabolic disorders.211 Ablation of Bmal1 nearly abolishes diurnal oscillations in total fecal microbial load and abundance across phyla and genera, accompanied by a shifted community configuration.212 Nfil3 deletion alters microbiota composition and induces energy metabolism imbalance, identifying the host clock–NFIL3 axis as a key hub for microbiota-host interactions.184 Furthermore, IEC-specific Bmal1 knockout disrupts microbial oscillations,213 demonstrating that the intestinal epithelial clock drives microbial rhythmicity.

In summary, the gut microbiota is closely associated with the components of the host circadian clock. The gut microbiota and its metabolites can modulate the expression of rhythmic genes in the host, while the host circadian rhythm also profoundly shapes the composition of the gut microbiota. Meanwhile, the gut microbiota itself exhibits corresponding circadian oscillations, and the bidirectional crosstalk between these oscillations and the host circadian rhythm exerts a profound impact on host health.

sec.title: The diet–microbiome–circadian interface: a dynamic network governing metabolic homeostasis and disease
sec.p: Diet plays a central role in the microbiome-circadian rhythm communication network.234 Diet serves as a pivotal driver in shaping the composition and function of the gut microbiota,85 profoundly influencing its ecological structure. In turn, the gut microbiota plays a distinctive role in transducing dietary signals to regulate circadian oscillations in host metabolic pathways.9 Through their metabolites, gut microbes impact host tissues and mediate rhythmicity in the host transcriptome, underscoring that circadian rhythms form the foundation of numerous host–microbe interactions.10 The circadian rhythms of both the gut microbiota and the host are integral to organismal health; indeed, disorders such as gastrointestinal diseases have been linked to disruptions in circadian rhythms accompanied by alterations in microbial composition.10 Thus, dietary patterns and components are crucial for synchronizing the host clock with microbial homeostasis and for preventing chronic diseases.

Diet exerts a crucial role in the management of diseases. Intermittent fasting (IF) demonstrates broad efficacy against conditions including obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, cancer, and neurological disorders.235 Mechanistically, IF restores the diurnal oscillation of key metabolic regulators in the liver—cAMP Response Element-Binding Protein ([CREB] phosphorylation-enhanced during fasting), mTOR (activated during feeding), and AMPK (promoting fatty acid oxidation)—and enhances the amplitude of clock gene expression. This synchronization of central and peripheral circadian clocks mitigates the negative impacts of rhythm disruption.236-238 Rhythmic clock gene expression is a prerequisite for normal physiological function, and distinct dietary patterns and compositions differentially modulate this expression.239

Dietary patterns can be temporally categorized into feeding and fasting phases. Their health impacts are mediated through energy-sensing molecular pathways: Feeding activates the insulin-pAKT-mTOR pathway, driving anabolic gene expression, while sustained fasting activates AMPK, triggering catabolic and repair processes.240,241 These patterns also regulate the molecular clock: During fasting, AMPK phosphorylates the circadian repressor CRY, targeting it for degradation, thereby relieving its inhibition of CLOCK: BMAL1 target genes (including Rev-erbα, Per, and Cry). Conversely, feeding activates mTOR, which indirectly modulates Per expression and clock gene activity.236 Evidence from murine models demonstrates that time-restricted feeding (TRF) corrects disrupted circadian rhythms and protects against obesity, fatty liver, insulin resistance, hyperinsulinemia, and inflammation. These beneficial metabolic effects are attributed to the fine-tuning of clock genes in response to metabolic regulation via mTOR, CREB, and AMPK pathways.236,242

Furthermore, these feeding patterns and dietary composition drive significant alterations in the gut microbiota composition. Specific dietary components substantially reshape the gut microbiota. For instance, high-fat diets alter microbial composition by increasing the abundance of Firmicutes while decreasing Bacteroidetes and Lactobacillus.243,244 In humans, high-protein intake generally elevates the relative abundances of Firmicutes, Proteobacteria, Lactobacillus, and Bifidobacterium, whereas Bacteroidetes and certain Clostridium species may decline.245 In mice, a high-protein, low-fiber diet induces marked microbial changes, characterized by reduced diversity, increased levels of Bacteroidaceae (including Bacteroides and Parabacteroides), and decreased proportions of Lachnospiraceae and Ruminococcaceae.246 Conversely, high-fiber diets significantly enrich Bifidobacterium, Bacteroides, and Prevotella.247 Western-style diets—high in calories, fat, and protein—promote an increase in Bacteroides abundance but concurrently reduce overall microbial diversity.248

Dietary patterns also have the ability to modulate the composition of the gut microbiota. Table 3 summarizes the corresponding changes in microbiota under different dietary patterns. TRF can alter the composition of the gut microbiota at specific time points; specifically, the abundance of Bacteroidetes in TRF mice at certain time points is higher than that in ad libitum-fed mice, whereas the abundance of Firmicutes is relatively lower.249

caption.p: Alterations in gut microbiota composition induced by dietary patterns.

sec.p: In addition, TRF helps maintain robust circadian rhythms and has been repeatedly demonstrated to prevent and even reverse metabolic imbalances in both mice and humans.250
This function relies on the unique capacity of the gut microbiota to transduce dietary cues to regulate the diurnal oscillations of host metabolic functions.9

Disrupted feeding rhythms abolish the diurnal oscillations of specific gut microbes (e.g., Parabacteroides distasonis). Such microbial alterations impair the release of the flavonoid metabolite glycitein (GLY), which regulates the inflammatory rhythms of rheumatoid arthritis (RA) via the SIRT5-NF-κB signaling axis. The deficiency of GLY leads to rhythmic bursts of proinflammatory cytokines,251 thereby compromising host health.

The microbiota exerts multi-dimensional regulatory mechanisms on circadian rhythm modulation. A robust microbiome stabilizes intestinal circadian cycles, promoting organismal synchrony and resilience against rapid environmental changes.252 Studies in GF mice demonstrate that microbial absence induces morphological alterations in IECs,253,254 and alters gene expression, including antimicrobial and circadian rhythm genes.255 GF mice exhibit significantly impaired expression of central and hepatic circadian clock genes (in the hypothalamus and liver) and fail to gain weight comparably to CV mice.160 GF mice exhibit arrhythmic expression of Bmal1 and Clock, while ABX mice show dysregulated Cry2 and a phase‑inverted rhythm of Bmal1, with its peak shifted from the dark to the light phase.170 Dysbiosis disrupts immune competence and circadian rhythms.256 Crucially, circadian clock disruption exacerbates immune dysfunction, tilting the balance towards a pro-inflammatory state and increasing susceptibility to immune-mediated diseases.188,257 These perturbations extend beyond mere clock gene dysregulation: they manifest in the physiological deficits—outlined above—driven by clock disruption and underscore that the microbiota modulates host circadian timing to impact health.

The microbiota also entrains the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis. Microbial depletion disrupts clock gene rhythms and abolishes normal HPA-axis oscillations, leading to arrhythmic glucocorticoid secretion.170 Conversely, a functional clock shapes the diurnal adhesion of specific taxa—such as Segmented filamentous bacteria (SFB) and Akkermansia—to regulate epithelial Major Histocompatibility Complex Class II (MHC II) expression in the small intestine, thereby controlling IL-10 production by CD4+ intraepithelial lymphocytes and maintaining barrier rhythmicity. Microbiota disruption or Per1/2 DKO abolishes the diurnal expression of MHC II+ surface-like IECs258; in Per1/2 DKO mice, altered feeding rhythms likely reshape microbial composition, further compounding clock dysfunction.258 Moreover, feeding rats with Panax ginseng extract alters their gut microbiota in a manner that induces upregulation of host clock genes in epithelial cells and yields anti-inflammatory effects via inhibition of the PI3K–Protein Kinase B (AKT) pathway.259 The interaction between the gut microbiota and host circadian rhythms is also crucial for health and metabolic diseases.260 For example, host circadian disruption combined with gut microbial dysbiosis can synergistically promote the development of obesity.260 Table 4 summarizes the changes in clock genes and gut microbial metabolic patterns in selected disease models. Targeting the gut microbiota, circadian clock regulation, and diet holds potential as a therapeutic strategy for these diseases. Certainly, aligning disease treatments with the circadian gene regulation–microbiota–metabolite axis can overcome pharmacoresistance in these diseases. In RA patients, disruption of this axis may allow inflammation to “escape” pharmacological control at unexpected time points. Adjusting drug administration times according to the intrinsic biorhythms of diseases (e.g., the inflammatory peaks of RA) to synchronize peak drug concentrations with the peaks of pathological activity may significantly improve therapeutic efficacy and reduce side effects.251 Currently, cutting-edge research in this field is exploring the specific molecular mechanisms. For instance, investigations are focused on how microbial metabolites “reprogram” immune cell functions through epigenetic modifications (e.g., histone methylation), and such reprogramming may affect the efficacy of drugs in immune-related diseases.261 Therefore, combining chronotherapy, microbiota-targeted interventions, and epigenetic-targeted drugs is expected to open up new avenues for addressing the pharmacoresistance challenge in these diseases.

caption.p: Roles of the circadian clock and gut microbiota in disease models and future perspectives.

tr.td: Colon mucosal biopsies showed upregulation of Bmal1 and downregulation of Per1/Per3.288 In intestinal epithelial cell-specific Bmal1 knockout mice, susceptibility to colitis was increased.289

Dysbiosis (e.g., a reduction in butyrate-producing bacteria) activates Toll-like receptors and the inflammasome, thereby amplifying the NF-κB/IL-17 signaling pathway.290 Circadian control of gut immune mediators (e.g. IL-17) is disrupted.290

Susceptibility to colitis was enhanced, with both DSS-induced acute colitis and IL-10 deficiency-associated chronic colitis exacerbated; additionally, the survival rate was significantly decreased.290

Chronotherapy (Dietary Intervention): Time-restricted eating (e.g., nighttime fasting) restored the rhythm of BMAL1 in intestinal epithelial cells, enhanced rhythmic fluctuations of the gut microbiota, and significantly alleviated colitis symptoms in mice.
Under physiological conditions, SCFAs, particularly butyrate, inhibit HDACs in intestinal epithelial cells and promote the differentiation of regulatory T cells (Tregs).291
Exacerbated mucosal inflammation, impaired intestinal barrier function, and aberrant migration of colonic immune cells (disrupted balance between Th17 cells and regulatory T cells).290
Clock modulation: Pharmacologic targeting of clock genes (e.g. REV-ERB agonizts) is being explored.

The disruption of circadian rhythms in the intestinal epithelium exacerbates colitis, reduces the abundance of SCFA-producing microbiota, and activates the pro-inflammatory STING signaling pathway.289 Circadian rhythm disruption exacerbates acute flares of IBD.289
Microbiome therapies: Probiotic and prebiotic strategies to boost SCFA levels.
Deregulation of circadian transcription factors (i.e., RORa, nocturnin) is implicated in Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease (NAFLD) progression to Non-Alcoholic Steatohepatitis (NASH)

The gut microbiota ferments dietary fiber to produce SCFAs such as butyrate and propionate. Butyrate exerts anti-inflammatory effects and enhances insulin sensitivity. Gut microbial dysbiosis leads to a decrease in SCFAs, which in turn promotes the development of metabolic disorders.292,293

Circadian rhythm disruption induces metabolic disorders (e.g., insulin resistance, dysregulated lipid metabolism). Increased intestinal permeability and endotoxin translocation exacerbate hepatic inflammation. Disruption of the hepatocyte circadian clock promotes fat accumulation and oxidative stress, which in turn accelerates the progression of systemic inflammation and fibrosis.292
Microbial intervention: Probiotics/prebiotics regulate SCFAs and restore circadian rhythms.Clock modulation: Pharmacologic targeting of clock genes (e.g. REV-ERB agonizts) is being explored.

The gut microbiota converts primary bile acids into secondary bile acids, which act as ligands to activate the FXR. FXR activation inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis and lipogenesis, thereby improving insulin sensitivity. Gut microbial dysbiosis alters the composition of the bile acid pool, impairs FXR signaling, and promotes the development of NAFLD.294
Metabolic disorders directly induce hepatic steatosis, oxidative stress, and inflammation in hepatocytes, thus driving the progression from simple fatty liver to NASH.295
Clinical trial direction: Research on personalized NAFLD therapy integrated with microbiome and circadian clock biomarkers

ClockΔ¹⁹ mutant mice: exhibiting defective islet β-cell function and susceptibility to diet-induced obesity.296 Mice with systemic knockout of the Bmal1 or Clock gene exhibit hyperglycemia, glucose intolerance, and obesity.297

The gut microbial diversity is decreased in patients with T2D mellitus (T2DM). The microbiota composition is altered: the abundance of butyrate-producing bacteria (e.g., Faecalibacterium prausnitzii) is reduced, while that of pathogenic bacteria is increased. Concomitantly, the levels of SCFAs and the synthesis of secondary bile acids are diminished, which impairs the FXR/TGR5 signaling pathway.298

Defects in pancreatic islet β-cell function (insufficient insulin secretion and exocytosis impairment), abnormal rhythmicity of hepatic glucose output, and disrupted peripheral insulin sensitivity. Animal models (ClockΔ¹⁹, Bmal1⁻/⁻) exhibit hyperglycemia and impaired glucose tolerance.296

Optimization of Chronotherapy: Adjust the administration time of insulin and glucose-/lipid-lowering drugs according to the individual’s circadian rhythm, so as to match the endogenous metabolic rhythm, enhance therapeutic efficacy, and reduce adverse effects.
Cry1/Cry2 double-knockout mice: Impairment of the core clock repressive arm leads to hepatic lipid accumulation, which indirectly disrupts metabolic homeostasis.296

The gut microbiota ferments dietary fiber to produce SCFAs such as butyrate and propionate. Butyrate exerts anti-inflammatory effects and enhances insulin sensitivity. Gut microbial dysbiosis leads to a reduction in SCFAs, which in turn promotes the development of metabolic disorders.292,293
Obesity and lipid metabolism disorders: increased hepatic lipid synthesis and abnormal intestinal fat absorption lead to hypertriglyceridemia and fatty liver.296

Modulators of Core Clock Components: Development of molecules capable of stabilizing or fine-tuning the amplitude/phase of the core clock loop (e.g., CK1δ/ε inhibitors for stabilizing PER proteins), which is still in the preclinical exploration stage.
REV-ERBα knockout leads to increased lipid synthesis, whereas its overexpression reduces blood lipid levels.296

Glucose metabolism disorders: elevated hepatic glucose output and impaired peripheral glucose uptake and utilization. Lipid metabolism disorders: promotion of hepatic de novo lipogenesis and lipid accumulation.296

Dysregulation of core circadian clock genes (e.g., BMAL1, CLOCK, PER, CRY) is closely associated with the initiation, progression, and prognosis of various cancers. Circadian rhythm disruption induces endocrine imbalance, which in turn impairs immune function and perturbs the tumor microenvironment

Dysbiosis of the gut microbiota is associated with the initiation and progression of various cancers such as colorectal cancer. The microbiota modulates immune cells in the tumor microenvironment (e.g., CD8⁺ T cells, Tregs, TAMs, MDSCs) via their metabolites (SCFAs, bile acids, tryptophan metabolites), promotes vascular remodeling and inflammatory responses, and thereby affects tumor progression.299

SCFA Pathway: Modulates AMPK/p38 MAPK/PGC1α signaling via receptors such as GPR41/43/109A, thereby regulating immunity and metabolism.299Bile Acid Pathway: Modulates GLP-1 secretion, immune regulation and metabolic homeostasis via receptors such as TGR5.299 Tryptophan Metabolism Pathway: Modulates cytokines including IL-22 via the AhR receptor, thereby affecting mucosal immunity and tumor immunity.299Inflammatory Pathway: Signaling pathways such as NF-κB, TLR and STAT3 play a critical role in microbiota-immunity-tumor interactions.299 A disrupted circadian-microbiota axis creates a pro-carcinogenic microenvironment, with taurocholic acid serving as a key mediator A.300

Chronotherapy: Design the administration time of chemotherapy/immunotherapy according to the patient’s circadian rhythm (termed as “chronomodulated immunotherapy”).Probiotic supplementation: Probiotics such as Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, and Akkermansia muciniphila can ameliorate gut microbial dysbiosis and enhance the efficacy of immunotherapy.Dietary intervention: A high-fiber diet or short-chain fatty acid analogs helps facilitate the anti-inflammatory circadian clock signaling pathway.Microbiota-immune combination therapy: e.g., probiotic supplementation combined with anti-PD-1 therapy.

In patients with RA, TNF-α induces the overexpression of the clock gene BMAL1.301The expression of cardiac clock genes (CLOCK, BMAL1, PER, CRY) is dysregulated in RA patients, with BMAL1 and PER2 being the most significantly affected.302

After the gut microbiota is depleted with antibiotics, the regulatory effect of feeding time on the rhythm of inflammation is abolished. Mice that receive fecal microbiota transplantation from the morning feces of RA patients exhibit more severe inflammation.251

Symptoms of RA patients (e.g., joint pain and swelling) are exacerbated in the morning and gradually alleviate during the daytime. Serum inflammatory factors (IL-6, TNF-α) increase from nighttime to early morning and decrease during the daytime.251

Regular meal timing, especially when restricted to the active phase, may help alleviate the dysregulation of inflammatory rhythms in RA.Supplementation with Parabacteroides distasonis or engineered probiotics expressing β-glucuronidase (e.g., EcN-β-GC) can increase GLY levels and reduce inflammation.Targeting genes such as BMAL1: Modulating their expression may serve as a novel therapeutic strategy for RA.

sec.p: In addition to diet acting as a zeitgeber, temperature also serves as an important zeitgeber signal. However, in the same region, ambient temperatures exhibit distinct variations across different seasons, which is why seasonal rhythms are particularly crucial in relevant research. The incidence of certain diseases fluctuates with seasonal changes; for instance, insulin sensitivity in patients with T2D displays a seasonal pattern (with lower sensitivity in winter), which may be partially attributed to gut microbiota-mediated alterations in short-chain fatty acid levels and intestinal barrier function.262 Furthermore, biologically plausible season-dependent associations exist between single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in the clock genes CRY1 and CRY2 and glucose homeostasis.263 This provides a novel perspective for understanding the seasonal fluctuations of T2D and implementing “season-personalized” health management strategies.

The gut microbiota also undergoes seasonal changes, with metabolic dysbiosis exacerbated during cold months (coupled with holiday dietary patterns). Seasonal variations in the gut microbiota may be involved in regulating the body’s seasonal energy balance: for example, cohort studies in some populations have indicated a higher Firmicutes/Bacteroidetes ratio in summer, which may facilitate fat storage, whereas “cold-tolerant” microbiota help promote lipolysis.262 This also suggests that seasonal factors need to be taken into account when designing microbiota-targeted intervention regimens.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The central pacemaker—the SCN—is well established for its role in generating and coordinating circadian rhythms, which are fundamental to organismal health. Diet serves as a potent zeitgeber that not only entrains biological rhythms but also engages in profound crosstalk with the gut microbiota. Accumulating evidence demonstrates that the gut microbiome significantly influences host physiology through multiple pathways and plays a precise role in maintaining systemic homeostasis. A central mechanism underlying this influence is its regulation of the host’s core clock genes. As an integral component of host immunity and digestion, the gut microbiota is intimately connected with dietary intake—the fundamental substrate of life—and the molecular circadian clock, which coordinates metabolism and immune function. The stability of three interrelated systems—dietary patterns, microbial ecology, and circadian regulation—forms an essential foundation for health. A deeper understanding of the diet–gut microbiota–circadian axis is therefore critical for elucidating the pathogenesis and treatment of related diseases, offering a dynamic new framework for interpreting disease heterogeneity and therapeutic resistance. In addition, seasonal rhythms represent another important factor influencing host health and gut microbial composition. Integrating both daily (circadian) and seasonal biological rhythms holds promise for advancing “chrono-precision medicine” and “microbiota-precision medicine.” Combining these approaches could provide novel therapeutic strategies for certain diseases and open new avenues to overcome treatment resistance. In summary, these lines of research establish circadian rhythmicity as a fundamental axis within the dynamic network connecting diet, gut microbiota, and host health.

sec.p: No potential conflicts of interest were disclosed.
